entry.title: Unable to configure outbound connectivity for API_PRODUCT_SRV in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

We are currently working on a requirement in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition related to Production Order documentation.

Business Requirement

The business needs to populate the inspection text maintained in the material master into the "Documents Libératoires" section of the Production Order form.

Since the inspection text is maintained on the product and needs to be available during production execution, we investigated several standard and extensibility options.

Analysis Performed

1. Text Determination

We initially explored standard Text Determination functionality to copy text from Material/BOM to the Production Order.

After discussions with the functional team, this approach was not suitable for retrieving the required inspection text.

2. CDS-Based Solution

We then analyzed available CDS views to retrieve the inspection text.

However, we could not identify a CDS view exposing this information, and SAP confirmed that no suitable CDS view is currently available.

3. Custom Field Workaround

As an alternative, we created a custom field to store the required inspection information so that it would be available through CDS-based reporting and extensions.

Unfortunately, the value is not consistently updated after saving the product, making this approach unreliable.

4. SAP Recommended Approach

SAP recommended retrieving the data through API_PRODUCT_SRV.

We therefore started implementing an outbound integration scenario.

Technical Steps Performed

Development

  1. Created package Z_DEV_API_SUB_PKG
  2. Defined outbound service ZINS_OUT_SRV_REST
  3. Created communication scenario ZINSP_CS
  4. Downloaded the EDMX metadata from SAP Business Accelerator Hub
  5. Generated Service Consumption Model ZINSP_TEXT_SCM_1
  6. Generated proxy class ZCLINSP_TEXT_SCM_1
  7. Developed ABAP console application ZCL_INSPECTION_TEXT

Configuration

  1. Created communication user CPI_PP_PROD
  2. Created communication system CPI_PRODUCT_INTEGRATION
  3. Started configuring the communication arrangement

Current Issue

During the outbound connectivity configuration, we are unable to identify how to create or assign the required outbound user for the API call.

This is preventing us from completing the integration based on API_PRODUCT_SRV.

Questions

  1. Has anyone successfully consumed API_PRODUCT_SRV from custom ABAP in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition?
  2. What is the correct approach for configuring outbound connectivity and authentication in this scenario?
  3. Is there a standard communication scenario that should be used instead of a custom one?
  4. How should the outbound user be created or assigned when consuming API_PRODUCT_SRV from ABAP Cloud?
  5. Is there an alternative recommended approach to retrieve inspection text maintained in the product master?

Any guidance, configuration examples, or lessons learned would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you for your support.

Best regards.

entry.title: Defect Correction Status is not being Update
entry.content:
Hello

I am experiencing an issue with the defect correction process in SAP Cloud ALM.

When a defect is created and later resolved by the IT team in the Defects application, they change the defect status to "Failure, Test Necessary" so that the tester can re-execute the test and verify the correction.

However, this status change is not automatically reflected in the corresponding Test Execution. As a result, the IT team must manually update the status in both applications (Defects and Test Execution), creating unnecessary rework and increasing the risk of inconsistencies.

Could you please clarify whether this is the expected behavior? If not, is there any configuration or standard functionality that enables synchronization between Defect and Test Execution statuses?

entry.title: How to enforce mandatory Change Number for BOM changes in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition?
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

We are implementing SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition and have a business requirement to ensure that all BOM changes can only be performed when a Change Number (Engineering Change Management) is assigned.

Business Requirement

Our customer requires full traceability of BOM changes.

Therefore, whenever a Material BOM is modified, users must enter a valid Change Number. Changes performed directly on a BOM without a Change Number should be blocked and should not be saved.

Current Configuration

  • Engineering Change Management is in use.
  • History Requirement is activated for the BOM usage.
  • We investigated the authorization objects:
    • C_STUE_NOC
    • C_STUE_NOH

Both are described as authorizations related to processing BOMs without a Change Number.

Following SAP recommendations, we attempted to restrict these authorizations in the business role.

What We Tried

  1. Identified the relevant BOM restriction types.
  2. Changed the role access from Unrestricted to Restricted with the value NO (N) selected
    (CF. photo)
  3. Set C_STUE_NOC to .
  4. Verified authorizations using:
    • Display Restriction Types
    • IAM Information System
    • Display Authorization Trace
  5. Reassigned the role and performed new tests.

Issue

Despite these restrictions:

  • The system still allows users to modify the BOM without entering a Change Number.
  • Only a warning message is displayed.
  • The BOM changes can still be saved successfully.

Therefore, the behavior remains different from the expected business requirement.

Questions

  1. Has anyone successfully enforced mandatory Change Numbers for BOM changes in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition?
  2. Are C_STUE_NOC and C_STUE_NOH sufficient to block BOM changes without a Change Number?
  3. Is there any additional configuration required (History Requirement, ECM settings, BOM usage settings, etc.)?
  4. Has SAP changed the behavior so that the warning cannot be converted into a hard error?
  5. Are there alternative approaches used by other customers to enforce this requirement?

We already tested the authorization-based approach suggested by SAP, but it did not prevent users from saving BOM changes without a Change Number.

Any insights or implementation experience would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you.

Best regards.

Usually the proxy-DB dies a sudden death without any notification. But I never looked at the console log of the main-DB. I will try it.

entry.title: Re: Partnerbanktyp aus F-47 nicht im F110 Echtlauf
entry.content:
Hallo,

dieses Verhalten tritt normalerweise auf, wenn in den Stammdaten des Kreditors zwei Bankverbindungen hinterlegt sind.

Dabei ist jede Bankverbindung einem eigenen Partnerbanktyp zugeordnet. Beispielsweise:

  • Partnerbanktyp 001 → Bankverbindung A
  • Partnerbanktyp 002 → Bankverbindung B

Standardmäßig verwendet das System die Bankverbindung, die dem Partnerbanktyp 001 zugeordnet ist. Soll die Zahlung jedoch über die Bankverbindung mit dem Partnerbanktyp 002 erfolgen, muss dieser Partnerbanktyp im Beleg manuell eingetragen werden.

Wird im Beleg kein Partnerbanktyp angegeben, ermittelt das System automatisch die Bankverbindung des Partnerbanktyps 001 und verwendet diese für die Zahlung.

entry.title: From Champions to Scale – How to Operationalize Joule for Consultants Across Project Teams
entry.content:
Reading time: 5–7 minutes

Having a handful of enthusiastic Joule for Consultants users is a good start, but it is not the same as adoption. In this blog, we share practical metrics, common adoption patterns, and proven scaling practices for SAP Practice Managers, Team Leaders, and Chief AI Officers looking to maximise ROI from their J4C investment.

 

The Real Challenge Starts After the Initial Pilot

Across the market, expectations around AI have become more pragmatic. Customers and partners no longer look for experimentation—they expect tangible value during implementation delivery. Joule for Consultants (J4C) is the practical AI assistant from SAP that already supports faster delivery and improved productivity in project execution.

In our previous blog, Joule for Consultants Adoption in Partner SAP Practices, we described J4C adoption as a leadership journey moving from cautious pilots to sustained, high-usage delivery. 

SAP partners who already started with J4C already will know that the real challenge is not creating a few champions or early enthusiasts. The difficult part is scaling their success into a consistent way of working across project teams. That step does not happen organically—it requires measurement, deliberate action, and structural change. 

We will look at four key areas:

  • How to measure adoption using the right metrics
  • How to turn those metrics into actionable insights
  • How new J4C capabilities support scaling
  • What a practical scaling path looks like

 

Making Real Usage Visible: Adoption Metrics

Scaling cannot be managed without data. To understand adoption, SAP provides J4C consumption data via SAP for Me, including user counts, generated messages, and AI unit consumption. 

However, raw data alone is not enough. Evaluating key metrics in context enables meaningful interpretation and better discussions with partners. Here is how we look at the adoption data in the context of an SAP practice:

Metric

Definition

SAP Practice Size

Number of SAP practitioners in the partner's SAP practice, as stated in the partner business plan

J4C Provisioned Users

Users assigned as eligible to consume AI Units through J4C — this represents the entitled user base

J4C Active Users

The subset of provisioned users who generated at least one message (prompt) in a given month — the metric that reflects actual usage

Provisioned Users (% of Practice)

Share of provisioned users relative to total practice size; enables benchmarking across partners of different sizes and measures how broadly J4C is embedded in the organization

Ratio Active Users vs Provisioned Users

Reveals the utilization of J4C subscriptions. If this ratio is low, the company is paying for unused access.

Messages per Month

Total number of interactions submitted by all users in a given month

Messages per Active User

Average number of messages per active user; indicates engagement intensity — are users working with J4C regularly or only occasionally?

Projects with J4C

How many implementation projects are actively supported by J4C, and in which areas?

Active Users per Project

How intensively J4C is used within a specific project — a critical signal because real value comes not from ad hoc use by enthusiasts, but from deliberate usage embedded in delivery

Additional project-team metrics, such as task or prompt completion rates, reduction in support / helpdesk requests or perceived productivity gains, can be captured manually to help validate expected productivity improvements.

Scaling is not about how many champions exist—it is about how consistently J4C is used across SAP teams and customer projects. These metrics provide a clear view of adoption maturity and readiness to generate ROI. 

 

What the Data Tells You: From Insight to Practice

Metrics alone are not enough—they only become valuable when translated into insights and action. There are a few typical patterns, which we see quite consistently, so let’s discuss what they mean.

Pattern 1: Large gap between provisioned users and active users

When active users are far below the provisioned base, the issue is structural: the partner is consuming AI Units but not realizing value from much of the investment. Scaling has happened commercially, but not operationally. Inactive users reduce ROI.

Tactical actions: Gather feedback from provisioned users, enable them on role- and project-specific use cases, and reassign licenses from chronically inactive users to colleagues more likely to benefit.

Best practice — Feedback loops: Use a simple recurring survey and ask provisioned users to submit   weekly feedback. Users who do not answer can lose their license allocation. And if there is negative feedback, sharing it with SAP helps inform the product backlog.

Best practice — License rotation: Some partners replace users who do not engage meaningfully, keeping momentum and consumption active across the provisioned pool.

Pattern 2: Active user count declining, but messages per active user increasing

This pattern suggests a committed core group is emerging - fewer people are experimenting, but those who remain have found valuable use cases and rely on J4C regularly.

Tactical actions: Learn from these users and share their prompt libraries, workflows, and success stories through team meetings, internal channels, or a shared prompt library.

Best practice — J4C included into the delivery toolchain: Practices that scaled J4C embedded it into formal delivery — proposals, project kick-offs, and the standard project toolset. Making it official and announced to customers, accelerates adoption beyond individual enthusiasm.

Pattern 3: Downward trend in both active users and messages

A decline in both metrics usually means licenses were assigned to the wrong users or teams lack sufficient visibility and enablement.

Tactical actions: Reevaluate and reassign licenses with the project view in mind — the goal is consistent usage in delivery, not broad distribution as a benefit. SAP partner manager can support this with anonymized benchmarking across similar partners.

Best practice — run a formal pilot: Do not leave adoption to chance. Set up a formal pilot with a defined leader and team, then systematically explore use cases and manage adoption.

Pattern 4: No data visible to practice leadership

If no one tracks adoption data, the likely issue is missing ownership: the business case was approved, but accountability for usage did not continue after purchase.

Tactical action: Appoint a J4C “champion” to review adoption data regularly and escalate issues. Your partner manager can provide guidance on accessing consumption data in SAP for Me.

Pattern 5: Solid active user base, but usage concentrated on a few projects

When individual adoption is healthy, but project coverage is narrow, motivated consultants are using J4C on their own initiative, but it is not yet part of project design or staffing.

Best practice — Embed J4C in project design: Successful partners budget J4C support at the proposal stage instead of leaving adoption to individual choice during execution. The focus shifts from “who wants a license?” to “which projects require J4C support?”—a key step toward operational maturity.

Use your data to identify the pattern that best fits your situation, then make a proactive management decision rather than waiting passively.

A note for partners yet to start a pilot: If your practice has not yet initiated a J4C pilot, SAP offers co-funding via Development Funds to help eligible partners offset their initial investment: Explore Joule for Consultants Initiative 2026

 

What's New in J4C: Features Supporting Scaling

J4C continues to evolve, with new capabilities shaped by partner feedback and real-world usage. 

At SAP Sapphire 2026, SAP introduced new J4C features and outlined the roadmap through early 2027. You can watch the session recording here, or see the continuously updated list in the Joule section on the SAP Help Portal.

Recent enhancements fall into two categories: individual productivity improvements and features designed to enable team-wide scaling.

User experience improvements: J4C now supports up to 50 active conversations, making it easier to manage several client, project, or topic-specific threads in parallel. A new Retry button allows users to retry, refine, shorten, or expand responses and edit original prompts without restarting the conversation. These improvements may appear incremental, but friction is the most common reason active users lapse.

Expert Workspace: Now generally available, Expert Workspace allows creation of personalized AI configurations tailored to a specific client, workstream, or domain — with custom instructions, reference files, and persistent context across conversations.

Peer-to-Peer Expert Sharing: Consultants can share Experts with colleagues, giving each teammate an independent copy to adapt to their own project context. This helps move effective configurations from individual power users to wider teams without administrative overhead.

Centralized Expert Library (Administrators): Administrators can curate and publish Expert configurations for all users, making senior consultants’ best workflows available practice wide. This supports standardization and helps new or less frequent users start from tested examples.

Custom Knowledge Grounding: Administrators can connect J4C to internal repositories such as Microsoft SharePoint, SAP Build Work Zone, or AWS S3 via SAP AI Core document grounding. This enables answers that combine company-specific assets, reusable project material, and SAP’s curated knowledge.

The H2 2026 roadmap also includes SAP Landscape Awareness, with a pilot starting in June 2026. This capability is intended to help J4C understand the customer’s system landscape and provide more personalized, context-aware and project-specific guidance for consultants.

Together, these capabilities help scale J4C beyond individual champions by making effective practices easier to reuse, share, govern, and embed into everyday project delivery.

 

Closing: From Productivity to Operational Excellence

The shift from individual champions to consistent project-level usage determines whether J4C becomes a competitive advantage or remains an isolated productivity tool. It requires structured management, clear accountability, and integration into delivery processes. 

Key actions to take:

  • Track the right metrics
    Monitor provisioned users, active users, engagement levels, and project coverage regularly
  • Diagnose before acting
    Identify your current adoption pattern and tailor actions accordingly
  • Scale what works
    Use Expert Sharing and libraries to institutionalise successful workflows
  • Embed J4C into project design
    Plan usage at proposal stage and align with delivery structures
  • Stay engaged with SAP
    Leverage feedback channels, pilot programmes, and benchmarking to accelerate adoption

Ultimately, organisations that capture the full value of Joule for Consultants will be those that convert individual productivity gains into a repeatable delivery model. 

 

Final note: Content of this article has been supported by Joule for Consultants. Wording and sentences have been refined using AI.

Here are examples of J4C prompts used to write this blog:

  • Which new J4C features have been made available in 2026?
  • How does J4C support team collaboration and on an implementation project?
  • Suggest how an SAP S/4HANA migration project manager should measure J4C adoption within the project team. Purpose is to evaluate and improve productivity gains in an implementation project. Use SAP best practices and propose simple, readily available, high-impact metrics.
  • Consultants of an SAP partner are using J4C with their own internal subscriptions. Where can this partner see data about their J4C / AI Unit usage? Not at customers, but his own consumption. Be specific and include navigation instructions.

entry.title: SAP Business AI Platform: What It Means for SAP BTP & SAP BDC Customers
entry.content:
At SAPPHIRE 2026, SAP unveiled the SAP Business AI Platform (BAIP) — a unified portfolio bringing together SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP), SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC), and SAP Business Transformation Management (BTM), enriched with new services and capabilities designed to make agentic AI a practical reality for SAP customers and partners.

In this session, we will recap the strategic positioning and vision behind SAP Business AI Platform, and explore what this evolution means specifically for SAP BTP and SAP BDC customers — both from a strategic perspective (how BAIP reshapes the platform landscape and investment priorities) and from a practitioner’s perspective (what changes operationally, technically, and day-to-day for architects, developers, and platform teams).

Register: https://events.sap.com/baip/en_us/home.html

Live on YouTube: https://youtube.com/live/6r2MNgbrT4Q
